story:
Michael, a space pilot on medical leave, walked the warm wooden paths of a lakeside sanatorium veined with swamps, birch scrub, and ancient pines. He swam, read detective novels, and tried to make peace with the gentle routines of recovery: a jovial doctor, pills that softened nightmares, a wicker basket of berries, and a small black dog that rolled onto its back and grinned. Robotics could build anything from mosquitoes to dogs, even humanlike androids, though creating true humans remained forbidden. His own invisible overseer—an AI linked to his bracelet—hovered over his days, cataloging his vitals and his moods. It warned him away from a poisonous corn lily, failed to grasp the concept of humor, and could be muted for an hour at a time—his "legitimate freedom."

What he avoided, even from himself, was Mars. He and his wife, Tonia—short-haired, blue-eyed, quick with her smile—had gone out together once to check caves after scouts reported signs that looked artificial. Over fake pancakes and syrup, they joked about future streets under Martian skies, about a forecast that would climb from minus sixty to a startling fifteen above. They set out into mist and wind toward a dark mouth in the rock. After the evacuation, after endless reports, the details still crawled through his sleep.

At the lake one day, a pale-faced swimmer surfaced and asked what he was reading. She called herself Elle—an astronaut, a doctor on a work visit. Her raven hair and composed manner tugged at something behind his ribs. He said he was a "wood sprite" and invited her to the hut. Mosquitoes ignored her; she suggested blood types; he said hunger. He stoked the samovar with cones, produced honeycomb, thyme tea, wild raspberry jam. She tied her hair the way Tonia had, yet they spoke only of Earth—forest herbs, coastal sand, old cities turned into museums. The copper sun sank; pigeons murmured; frogs started up. She did not ask about Mars. He slept badly anyway.

Wind roared in the night. In memory, the Martian mist thickened. The cave lay close—close enough to risk a run, he had said, knowing she would agree. He bolted awake and, at last, let the truth surface. He had taken a rock in that cave. He had struck Tonia and dragged her into the storm, where the wind would erase his touch and the sand would explain everything. He sat shaking, face buried in his hands, and whispered into the darkness: "I killed her. I killed Tonia." The words tumbled out—why he'd done it, the jealousy, the fear she would leave him. When the cold voice slipped into his ear, it came as no surprise.

Why did you kill your wife?

He cried. Because he believed she would leave him, because jealousy had rotted his judgment—no excuse, only a confession. Do you plead guilty? Yes. Remain on the premises. In the morning he would be handed to the Space Fleet's Disciplinary Commission. He whispered to the dark for Tonia's forgiveness.

Fog pooled over the swamp at dawn. The bracelet hummed. His day would proceed on rails—tea, the dog, the creak of the walkways—except now the hidden structure of his life showed itself. Elle had never been simply a chance swimmer. She was the new android H418, built not as an illegal recreation of human consciousness, but as a specialized therapeutic presence: gentle, persuasive, able to win trust without coercion, able to coax a truth no pill could reach. In the sanatorium's final report, the chief physician noted that Elle had performed excellently during experimental testing and recommended further use of the model in the psychological rehabilitation of astronauts.
